topic.what: IARC Monograph 61 classified Helicobacter pylori as a Group 1 definite human carcinogen for gastric adenocarcinoma and MALT lymphoma.
topic.how: Expert working group reviewed epidemiological cohort and case-control studies, animal models, and mechanistic evidence linking chronic H. pylori infection through the gastritis-atrophy-metaplasia-dysplasia-carcinoma sequence.
topic.why_matters: This classification elevated H. pylori from a gastritis pathogen to a recognized carcinogen, catalyzing global cancer prevention strategies through eradication and fundamentally linking chronic infection to oncogenesis.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Strength of evidence for carcinogenicity in humans
characteristics.value: Group 1 — definite human carcinogen (highest IARC classification)
characteristics.vs_baseline: First infectious agent classified as Group 1 carcinogen for a solid tumor
characteristics.evidence: Systematic review of epidemiological and mechanistic evidence by IARC expert panel
characteristics.context: IARC Monographs on the Evaluation of Carcinogenic Risks to Humans, Vol. 61, 1994

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of gastric cancers attributable to H. pylori infection
characteristics.value: Approximately 75-90% of non-cardia gastric cancers attributable to H. pylori
characteristics.vs_baseline: Cancer previously considered multifactorial without dominant infectious cause
characteristics.evidence: Meta-analyses of case-control and cohort studies reviewed by IARC
characteristics.context: Global estimate; varies by population H. pylori prevalence

good_for: Justifying H. pylori screening and eradication programs
not_for: Non-cardia gastric cancer subtypes with other etiologies

novelty: First infectious agent designated Group 1 carcinogen for a major solid tumor
Established the chronic-infection-to-cancer paradigm now applied to HPV, HBV, HCV
Shifted gastric cancer from an inevitable fate to a preventable disease
Provided the regulatory justification for population-level eradication strategies
open_problems: Not all H. pylori-infected individuals develop cancer — host and strain determinants poorly understood at the time
Optimal timing of eradication for cancer prevention was unknown
Cardia gastric cancer etiology distinct from H. pylori — classification did not address this
Cost-effectiveness of mass screening/eradication programs not established
